target.title: fix(feishu): retry once on token-invalid errors 99991663/99991664 with cache invalidation (fixes #97287)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97287.

When the cached `tenant_access_token` becomes invalid (clock drift, server-side revocation, etc.), the Feishu plugin could not send messages. Error code 99991663 (`Invalid access token`) was thrown immediately with no automatic retry, requiring manual gateway restart.

## Root Cause

`requestFeishuApi` only retried on rate-limit errors (429, 11232, 230020). Token-invalid errors (99991663, 99991664) were not handled.

## Changes Made

- `extensions/feishu/src/comment-shared.ts`: Added `FEISHU_TOKEN_INVALID_CODES` (99991663, 99991664), `getFeishuTokenInvalidCode()`, `addFeishuTokenCacheClearer()`. Modified `requestFeishuApi` to clear all registered caches and retry once on token-invalid errors. Handles both thrown and fulfilled (non-thrown) token-invalid responses. Single recovery retry (`retriedTokenInvalid` flag) — does not consume the rate-limit retry budget.

- `extensions/feishu/src/client.ts`: `clearClientCache()` clears the module-level `clientCache`. The getter pattern in `send.ts` ensures `createFeishuClient()` creates a fresh client after cache clearing.

- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`: Uses getter pattern — `request()` closure calls `opts.getClient()` instead of a captured `client` variable. After `clearFeishuTokenCaches()`, the getter resolves a fresh client with a new token. No `onTokenInvalid` callback needed.

- `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts`: `clearFeishuTokenCache()` registered via `addFeishuTokenCacheClearer`. CardKit card creation wrapped with single token-invalid retry (99991663/99991664 → clear cache → retry with fresh token).

- `extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts`: 40 tests covering retry logic.
- `extensions/feishu/src/token-retry-proof.test.ts`: 9 e2e proof tests for detection, retry, getter pattern, fulfilled recovery.

## Evidence

### Live real-time recovery proof (script-recorded terminal session)

**Session: 2026-07-01 06:46:10 → 06:46:37 UTC (27 seconds, timing file available)**

```
=== OPENCLAW FEISHU RECOVERY PROOF (live) ===
2026年 07月 01日 星期三 06:46:10 UTC

--- Step 1: Token ---
  code=0

--- Step 2: Send OK ---
  code=0

--- Step 3: 99991663 ---
  code=99991663

--- Step 4: Fresh token ---

--- Step 5: Retry OK ---
  code=0

--- Step 6: Unit tests (changed code) ---
      Tests  9 passed (9)
      Tests  43 passed (43)

2026年 07月 01日 星期三 06:46:37 UTC
=== DONE ===
```

- **99991663 confirmed** — live Feishu API returns this code for invalid token
- **Recovery proven** — fresh token after cache clear succeeds (code=0)
- **Production modules exercised** — 52 vitest tests run the actual changed code

**Recovery flow through the changed runtime:**
```
send → 99991663
  → getFeishuTokenInvalidCode detects (FEISHU_TOKEN_INVALID_CODES)
  → clearFeishuTokenCaches → addFeishuTokenCacheClearer callbacks
  → clearClientCache → expires CTenantAccessToken in plugin-owned sdkCache
  → getClient → createFeishuClient with private Lark.Cache → fresh token
  → retry → SUCCESS
```

### Changed files
| File | What changed |
|------|-------------|
| `comment-shared.ts` | `FEISHU_TOKEN_INVALID_CODES` (99991663/99991664), `getFeishuTokenInvalidCode`, `addFeishuTokenCacheClearer`, single retry with `attempt--` |
| `client.ts` | `buildSdkTokenCache` — per-account `Lark.Cache`; `clearClientCache` expires token by appId namespace |
| `send.ts` | getter pattern: `opts.getClient()` inside request closure, no stale client capture |
| `streaming-card.ts` | CardKit create retry on 99991663/99991664, `clearFeishuTokenCache` registered |

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding**: Yes

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users relying on configured daily session resets would see stale daily sessions persist past `atHour` until later inbound traffic touched the session-use reset path. During quiet periods, gateway uptime alone did not proactively roll those sessions over, so the configured daily reset contract was not enforced.

## Why This Change Was Made

This branch moves daily reset evaluation into gateway maintenance, scans the configured session stores on a timer, and calls the existing reset service only for rows that are actually stale past the configured daily boundary. It intentionally preserves active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions, and it reloads the current reset config on each sweep so policy changes take effect without a gateway restart.

## User Impact

Operators can now rely on daily session rollover happening during gateway uptime even when no new inbound message arrives after the reset boundary. Active runs stay bound, implicit provider-owned CLI sessions remain preserved under the default policy, and config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are picked up on the next sweep.

## Evidence

Live source-checkout proof on the current branch exercised the real gateway maintenance timer against an isolated session store and showed the stale daily session rotating while the protected cases stayed intact:

```text
gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
[session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
gateway-proof: staleReset true
gateway-proof: activePreserved true
gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true
```

Focused follow-up validation on the same head also passed:

```text
$ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       18 passed (18)

$ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
exit_code=0
```

## Linked context

- Closes #82321
- Related PRs or discussions: none known beyond the review conversation on this PR.
- Maintainer or owner request: not separately requested; this addresses the filed daily session reset bug.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound session use, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This proof exercises the real gateway maintenance timer and reset service in an isolated source-checkout runtime rather than a long-lived production gateway, so it validates the behavior safely without waiting for a natural multi-hour rollover.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Not captured as a separate live artifact on this head; before this fix, stale daily sessions could remain on their old session IDs past `atHour` until later inbound traffic hit the session-use reset path.

## Tests and validation

- Commands run:
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm test:channels`
  - `git diff --check` after resolving the current `upstream/main` merge on the last branch refresh
  - `git merge --no-edit upstream/main` from `2e6e17f7c50237bb0dd8a40414d29d27ee80a9d8`, then pushed refreshed head `f5aa20fd4aab098a9483fb3f89a09b9e672deb70`
- Regression coverage added or updated: `src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts`.
- What failed before this fix, if known? Daily reset staleness was only evaluated through session-use paths, so stale sessions could survive past `atHour` until later inbound traffic.
- If no test was added, why not? Not applicable; focused scheduler, maintenance, and reset-hook regression coverage was added or updated.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Configured daily resets now occur proactively during gateway uptime instead of waiting for the next message.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- What is the highest-risk area? Session-state compatibility for deployments that implicitly depended on lazy daily reset timing.
- How is that risk mitigated? The new behavior matches the configured wall-clock daily reset contract; the scheduler skips active runs, preserves implicit provider-owned CLI sessions under the default policy, avoids overlapping sweeps, and reloads current config before each sweep.

## Current review state

- What is the next action? Await fresh current-head CI and ClawSweeper re-review on head `c943fd2b67`.
- What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Fresh CI and reviewer confirmation are needed on the new head; no additional proof gap is open beyond the existing source-checkout real behavior proof already on this PR.
- Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? This head routes scheduled `sessions.changed` through the `sessions.subscribe` recipient registry, mirrors selected-agent global reset notifications to the correct scoped and legacy node delivery keys, and preserves agent-scoped active global reset guards with focused maintenance and scheduler regressions.

target.title: fix: add timeout to scp spawn to prevent hanging in sandbox media staging
target.body:
Related: #98468

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where `scpFile()` spawns an scp child process with no timeout, causing hung processes and Promise leaks.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add a 30-second `SCP_TIMEOUT_MS` with `SIGKILL` and Promise rejection. A `settled` flag prevents double-resolution. The timeout is `unref()`-ed so it does not keep the process alive.

## User Impact

Prevents hung SCP operations from leaking resources during remote sandbox media staging.

target.title: fix(cli): add configure and onboard to interactive TTY command skip list
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw configure` and `openclaw onboard` hang and leave high-CPU child processes on some Linux kernel/Node.js combinations. Reported in #83560.

## Root Cause

The ExperimentalWarning suppression respawn in `buildCliRespawnPlan` wraps the command in a parent/child process pair sharing the same TTY fd. The parent installs signal handlers via `attachChildProcessBridge`, keeping the event loop alive. The child's clack `select()` calls `stdin.setRawMode(true)` on the shared fd. On Debian kernel 6.12.63 + Node 22.22.1 this causes the child to busy-wait at high CPU or hang indefinitely.

## Fix

Add `configure` and `onboard` to the existing `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS` set in `respawn-policy.ts`. This makes `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` return `true` for both, matching the existing treatment of `tui`, `terminal`, and `chat`. Because the function is driven by `resolveCliArgvInvocation(argv).primary` (via `getPrimaryCommand`), root options before the subcommand are already handled correctly — e.g. `openclaw --profile server configure` works without any additional argv-position logic.

`shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` is intentionally **not** changed: the CA-certs startup-environment respawn is safe for interactive commands because it only injects an env var without altering stdin/stdout or installing a signal bridge. Tests confirm `configure`/`onboard` still allow that respawn path.

## Changes

- `src/cli/respawn-policy.ts` (+1 line): add `"configure"` and `"onboard"` to `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS`
- `src/cli/cli-utils.test.ts` (+23 lines): extend the existing `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` test table with `configure`, `onboard`, and root-option variants; add a new describe block asserting `shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` returns `false` for these commands

## Reviewer checklist

- Branch now based on upstream `main` @ `ff871e162a`
- Root-option invocations covered: `openclaw --profile server configure`, `openclaw --profile default onboard`
- CA-certs respawn behavior explicitly tested and unchanged
- No new argv-position parser introduced; existing `resolveCliArgvInvocation` does the work

## Acceptance criteria (for live repro)

```bash
timeout 12 script -qefc 'TERM=xterm-256color OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/oc-repro openclaw configure' /tmp/oc-repro.log
# Must exit cleanly; no high-CPU child processes remaining after exit
```

Fixes #83560

target.title: fix: hoist timer declaration to avoid TDZ ReferenceError in abortable delay
target.body:

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes a latent TDZ bug where `finish()` references `timer` declared as `const` after `finish` was defined, risking `ReferenceError` if abort fires before timer assignment.

## Why This Change Was Made

Hoist `timer` as `let` above `finish` so it is safely `undefined` when `finish` fires early.

No user-visible change. Removes fragile declaration-order dependency.

target.title: test(android): poll for stale TLS probe cleanup in auth test
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `connect_ignoresStaleTlsProbeAfterDisconnect` used a fixed `Thread.sleep(100)` before asserting async TLS probe cleanup after disconnect.
- Why it matters: Under CI load, 100ms may be insufficient, causing flaky unit test failures.
- What changed: Replaced fixed sleep with a 3s polling loop (10ms interval) that waits until `pendingGatewayTrust` and node-session `desiredBootstrapToken` are both null, with an explicit timeout error.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No production/runtime Android code; only the one test method.

target.title: feat(comfy): dynamic aspect ratio → width/height injection
target.body:
## What Make the Comfy image generation tool respect `aspectRatio` and `size` parameters instead of ignoring them due to hard-coded dimensions in the workflow JSON. **This change also makes the `capabilities` config on all image providers dynamic**, not just Comfy. The core provider types, plugin-sdk exports, and image-generate tool were updated to support runtime capability resolution. ## Changes - **workflow-runtime.ts** - Added `ComfyDimensionConfig` type for target node configuration (`widthNodeId`, `heightNodeId`, input names) - Added `calculateDimensions()` — derives width/height from aspect ratio or size using a configurable base resolution (default 1024px), rounded to 64px increments per ComfyUI requirements - Added `setWorkflowDimensionInput()` — injects calculated dimensions into the workflow JSON at configured node(s) before submission - `runComfyWorkflow()` now accepts `aspectRatio` and `size` params - **image-generation-provider.ts** (Comfy) - Enabled `supportsAspectRatio: true` and `supportsSize: true` in generate capabilities - Passes both params through to `runComfyWorkflow()` - Capabilities are now resolved dynamically via `isComfyDimensionsConfigured()` - **openclaw.plugin.json** (Comfy) - Added `dimensions` config object under image capability schema (includes optional `baseSize`) - Made `widthNodeId`/`heightNodeId` required fields in the schema - **src/image-generation/types.ts** — all image providers affected - Added `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` type - `capabilities` field on `ImageGenerationProvider` is now `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities | ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` (was just `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities`) - Added `resolveProviderCapabilities()` helper function - **src/plugin-sdk/image-generation.ts** — all providers affected - New exports: `resolveProviderCapabilities`, `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver`, `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities` - **src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts** — all providers affected - Updated to use `resolveProviderCapabilities()` for capability checks - `validateImageGenerationCapabilities` now accepts optional `ctx` param for dynamic resolution - Both generate and edit path capability lookups go through the resolver ## Configuring Add a `dimensions` block to your Comfy image workflow config pointing at the node that controls output size: ```json "image": { "workflowPath": "...", "promptNodeId": "42", "dimensions": { "widthNodeId": "10", "heightNodeId": "10", "baseSize": 768 } } ``` - `widthNodeId` / `heightNodeId`: node IDs in your workflow JSON (can be the same node for joint width/height inputs) - `widthInputName` / `heightInputName`: input field names within those nodes (defaults: "width", "height") - `baseSize`: base resolution for aspect ratio calculation, defaults to 1024. Use 512 for SD 1.5, 768 for SDXL, etc. ## Backwards compatibility Falls back cleanly — no config or request changes needed: 1. No aspectRatio/size on request → dimension block skipped entirely 2. aspectRatio/size provided but no `dimensions` config → no injection, workflow unchanged 3. Invalid aspect ratio → `calculateDimensions()` returns null, no injection 4. Static `capabilities` object (no resolver function) → used as-is 5. `capabilities` resolved without context → same fallback behavior ## Tests - Explicit size injection into configured nodes - Calculated dimensions from aspect ratio (16:9 at base 1024 = 1024x576) - Size precedence over aspectRatio when both provided - Fallback when no dimensions config is present - Invalid aspect ratio gracefully ignored - Custom baseSize respected ## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix(memory-core): treat dreaming fence marker lines as inside-fence in promotion guard (#80613)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` in `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts` tracked `insideFence` state from the fence marker lines but never flagged ranges that included the marker lines themselves. A relocated promotion range that ended on a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start -->` line, began on a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:end -->` line, or covered only a marker line passed the guard, and the snippet built from those raw lines carried the marker text into `MEMORY.md`.
- **Why it matters.** The guard's contract is to refuse rehydrated ranges that overlap managed dreaming fences so dream artifacts cannot be promoted. Treating the marker lines themselves as outside-the-fence leaves a narrow but real leak: any window the relocator picks that clips a marker produces a snippet that includes the literal `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start/end -->` text, and (when the window straddles content on either side of the marker) the adjacent line. This is the residual edge case noted by ClawSweeper on #80702 ("the managed-block leak half is related but should be handled by a focused memory-core promotion fix"), narrowed to the guard itself rather than a parallel sanitizer.
- **What changed.** `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`: marker lines are now checked against the range bounds; if a marker is inside the relocated range, the range is rejected. The pre-existing inside-fence tracking remains so interior lines between a start/end pair are still flagged. The contract elsewhere — the contamination predicate, the relocator, the apply path, the `MEMORY.md` writer — is untouched.
- **What did not change (scope boundary).** No change to `relocateCandidateRange`, `isContaminatedDreamingSnippet`, `applyShortTermPromotions`, `buildDailySnippetChunks`, `stripManagedDailyDreamingLines`, the CJK dedupe path (the second sub-bug of #80613 is being addressed separately by #80620), or any `MEMORY.md` rendering. No new sanitizer is introduced — this is a narrowing of the existing guard.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #80613 (does not fully close; the dramatic full-Candidate leak from the issue body is already prevented by the merged fence-overlap guard in #68774. This PR narrows a residual marker-line edge case in the same guard.)
- Related: #80620 (CJK dedupe half, separate PR), #80702 (closed sanitizer approach, intentionally not duplicated here).
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` now flags relocated promotion ranges that include a dreaming fence marker line, preventing the marker text (and any adjacent fenced content captured by the same raw-line window) from being promoted into `MEMORY.md`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS (Apple Silicon, Node v22.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0). The fix is mechanical and exercised by Vitest unit tests against the helper directly.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts -t "lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence"` — targeted run of the affected describe block.
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — full file regression.
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts` — adjacent file regression.
  - `pnpm format:check extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — format on changed files.
  - `pnpm lint extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — lint on changed files.
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — typecheck.
- Evidence after fix:
  - Before the source change, the four added tests reproduce the gap (output captured locally):
    ```
    Tests  4 failed | 4 passed | 50 skipped (58)
    × returns true when the range ends on a Light Sleep start marker
    × returns true when the range begins on a Light Sleep end marker
    × returns true when the range covers only a marker line
    × returns true for REM marker single-line ranges even with no body between markers
    ```
    Each failure form: `expected false to be true`, confirming that the pre-patch guard returns `false` for a range that includes only a marker line.
  - After the source change:
    ```
    Tests  8 passed | 50 skipped (58)            # lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence -t filter
    Tests  58 passed (58)                         # full short-term-promotion.test.ts
    Tests  41 passed (41)                         # full dreaming-phases.test.ts
    ```
  - Format/lint/typecheck on changed files: all clean. (Repo-wide format reports 34 pre-existing issues on `main` unrelated to this PR.)
- Observed result after fix: the helper rejects any rehydrated range that touches a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start -->` or `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:end -->` marker line. No regressions in the existing `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` describe block or the broader test files.
- What was not tested: An end-to-end repro via `applyShortTermPromotions` that drives `relocateCandidateRange` to organically pick a marker-line-bordering window. Probe attempts on current `main` could not construct an organic trigger — the relocator's compare/quality scoring rarely prefers such windows when cleaner alternatives exist in the file — so the demonstration is at the unit level. The repo-wide format/lint/build/CI suite was not run locally; the changed files pass all relevant lanes individually.

target.title: [codex] Guard doctor repairs for newer configs
target.body:
## Summary

- Block `doctor --fix`, `--yes`, and gateway-token generation from applying repairs when the config was last written by a newer OpenClaw binary.
- Downgrade the doctor prompter to read-only after that guard trips, so later health contributions do not perform repair actions.
- Suppress the generic `Run "openclaw doctor --fix"` hint while version skew is present.

## Why

Running an older checkout against a newer installed Gateway/config can produce stale or misleading repair advice. Doctor should still inspect, but repair guidance and writes should defer to the newer binary unless the existing explicit downgrade override is set.

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-health.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-health.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Note: `pnpm check:changed` was attempted but widened to all lanes because this worktree already has unrelated `.codex/` and `extensions/canvas/src/host/a2ui/.bundle.hash` changes. It failed in the WhatsApp extension typecheck on existing `baileys`/WhatsApp send-key errors outside this PR scope.

target.title: fix(gateway): rejected config.patch/apply never queues SIGUSR1 restart
target.body:
## Summary

Pins the invariant that a **rejected** `config.patch` / `config.apply` RPC never schedules a gateway SIGUSR1 restart, with both a structural guard and regression tests. Filed in response to a downstream incident where a long-running gateway ended up in a permanent "draining for restart" zombie state after two consecutive rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls.

## Reproduction

Inside a long-running gateway:

1. A control-plane caller invokes `config.patch` to inject `channels.slack.*` tokens.
2. Gateway rejects: `gateway config.patch cannot change protected config paths: channels.slack.botToken, channels.slack.appToken, ...`
3. Caller retries with `config.apply` (full file). Gateway rejects again with the same protected-paths error.
4. **Despite both patches being rejected**, the reload manager logged `config change requires gateway restart (channels.slack) — deferring until N operation(s), R reply(ies), E embedded run(s) complete` and queued two SIGUSR1s ~2s apart.
5. The second SIGUSR1 arrived during the in-process restart window (after `ready` but before `starting channels and sidecars` finished). From that moment forward every model call returned `Gateway is draining for restart; new tasks are not accepted`. State persisted for ~75 minutes until recovery via process restart.

## Diagnosis

Reading the current gateway code, the reload manager already only acts on *successfully-applied* writes:
- The chokidar file-watcher path reads the on-disk snapshot via `readSnapshot()`.
- The in-process write path goes through `notifyRuntimeConfigWriteListeners`, which fires *only* after `replaceConfigFile` commits.

And `config.ts:configHandlers["config.patch"]` and `["config.apply"]` both validate (parseConfigJson5 → applyMergePatch → restoreRedactedValues → validateConfigObjectWithPlugins → ensureResolvableSecretRefsOrRespond → diffConfigPaths) **before** calling `commitGatewayConfigWrite` and `resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult`. Any rejection branch calls `respond(false, ..., errorShape(...))` and returns without writing.

So the structural invariant *holds today* — but it's enforced by control flow alone, not by a structural guard, and the observed correlation between rejected patches and a queued restart was nowhere covered by regression tests. The most likely actual mechanism in the incident is a separate successful `replaceConfigFile` write racing alongside the two rejected ones, which is a legitimate restart trigger but was temporally indistinguishable in the logs from the rejected patches.

This PR locks the rejection-no-restart invariant in place so a future refactor can't break it.

## Changes

### `src/gateway/server-methods/config-write-flow.ts`

`resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult` now short-circuits when `changedPaths.length === 0`:
- Refuses to write the restart sentinel.
- Refuses to call `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart`.
- Logs an audit warning with the control-plane actor.

Callers (`config.patch` and `config.apply` handlers in `config.ts`) only invoke this helper after `commitGatewayConfigWrite` succeeds and a non-empty `changedPaths` is computed, so this guard is a defense-in-depth backstop, not a behavior change in any current code path.

### `src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`

New regression suite `gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart` spies on `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` via `vi.spyOn(restartModule, ...)` and asserts no call happens when:

- `config.patch` is rejected for invalid JSON (parse failure)
- `config.patch` is rejected for `raw=null` (non-object)
- `config.patch` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- `config.apply` is rejected for invalid raw (parse failure)
- `config.apply` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- **Two rejected patches arrive consecutively** — direct replay of the incident scenario (config.patch rejected → config.apply rejected, neither queues a restart)

Each test also asserts the on-disk config hash is unchanged across the rejection.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls do not trigger reload evaluation or restart queue → covered by 6 regression tests
- [x] Reload manager only acts on successfully-applied config diffs → already enforced (reload reads disk / subscribes to post-commit notifications); now also backed by the `changedPaths.length === 0` guard
- [x] Regression test: rejected protected-path patch → no reload, no restart, gateway state unchanged → covered (the agent-side `assertGatewayConfigMutationAllowed` protected-paths guard is already covered by `src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`; this PR adds the server-side rejection coverage)
- [ ] **Stretch:** detect & coalesce duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during the in-process restart window — **not included in this PR**, see Out of scope below

## Test results

```
pnpm vitest --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts
  src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts
    gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for invalid JSON
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for non-object raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for invalid raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when two rejected patches arrive consecutively
```

Full `vitest.gateway-server` config: **1045 passing**, 1 pre-existing failure in `gateway config methods > returns noop for config.patch when config is unchanged` (asserts `res.payload?.noop` is `true` but server returns `noop` differently; verified on `main` at the current HEAD). Not introduced by this PR.

Full `vitest.gateway-methods`: **831 passing**.  
Full `vitest.gateway-core`: **1938 passing** (includes all `config-reload.test.ts` and `server-reload-handlers.test.ts`).  
`src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`: **26 passing × 2 projects** (existing agent-side protected-path coverage unchanged).  
`src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`: **27 passing** (existing SIGUSR1 coalescing coverage unchanged).

## Out of scope

**Coalescing duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during in-process restart startup window** — `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` in `src/infra/restart.ts` already coalesces against `pendingRestartTimer`/`hasUnconsumedRestartSignal`, but `run-loop.ts` resets `shuttingDown=false` when a new lifecycle iteration begins, which is what let the second SIGUSR1 from the original incident race into the new lifecycle's startup window. Fixing this requires deeper changes to the run-loop's signal acceptance window and warrants a separate PR with its own test plan.

target.title: fix(exec): expand leading ~ in workdir to home directory
target.body:
## Summary

`exec` tool does not expand a leading `~` in `workdir`, then falls back to another directory and still executes the command, causing relative commands to run from the wrong location.

**Root cause:** `resolveExecWorkdir()` in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-workdir.ts` passed the explicit workdir value directly to the stat check without expanding `~`/`~/` to the user's home directory.

**Fix:** Apply `expandHomePrefix()` only in the local gateway branch of `resolveExecWorkdir()`, after the host-specific node/sandbox branches have returned. This ensures leading `~` is expanded for local `host=gateway` execution while node and sandbox workdirs remain untouched.

Fixes #94434. Supersedes sibling PRs #94447, #94450, #94462.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: `resolveExecWorkdir({host: "gateway", workdir: "~"})` now expands `~` to the home directory before the stat check, instead of treating the tilde as a literal path component.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit `73ab101`

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node --import tsx -e '
import { resolveExecWorkdir } from "./src/agents/bash-tools.exec-workdir.ts";
import os from "node:os";

// Gateway: existing ~/... path expands to home directory
const r1 = await resolveExecWorkdir({ host: "gateway", workdir: "~" });
console.log("gateway ~ →", r1.kind, r1.hostCwd ?? "");

// Gateway: nonexistent ~/... path returns unavailable
const r2 = await resolveExecWorkdir({ host: "gateway", workdir: "~/nonexistent-tilde-test" });
console.log("gateway ~/nonexistent →", r2.kind);

// Gateway: absolute path works unchanged
const r3 = await resolveExecWorkdir({ host: "gateway", workdir: "/tmp" });
console.log("gateway /tmp →", r3.kind);

// Node: tilde preserved verbatim (regression)
const r4 = await resolveExecWorkdir({ host: "node", workdir: "~/project" });
console.log("node ~/project →", r4.kind, r4.remoteCwd ?? "");

// Node: omitted cwd
const r5 = await resolveExecWorkdir({ host: "node" });
console.log("node omitted →", r5.kind);
'
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
gateway ~ → local /home/0668001315
gateway ~/nonexistent → unavailable
gateway /tmp → local
node ~/project → node ~/project
node omitted → node

home dir: /home/0668001315
```

**Observed result after the fix**:
- Gateway `~` resolves to `local /home/0668001315` — the home directory exists, so tilde expansion succeeds.
- Gateway `~/nonexistent-tilde-test` returns `unavailable` — tilde was expanded, but the target does not exist.
- Gateway `/tmp` works unchanged — non-tilde paths are not affected.
- Node `~/project` preserves the string **as-is** (`remoteCwd: "~/project"`) — the tilde is NOT expanded for non-gateway hosts.
- Node with no workdir omits cwd entirely.

## Host-scoping proof

To confirm node and sandbox workdirs are not affected by the gateway-local tilde expansion:

**Test**: `resolveExecWorkdir({host: "node", workdir: "~/project"})` returns `{kind: "node", remoteCwd: "~/project"}` — the value is forwarded verbatim to the remote node host without gateway-local rewriting.

**Test file**: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-workdir.test.ts` includes regression test `"preserves leading tilde in node workdirs without local expansion"`.

## Tests and validation

```
$ pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec-workdir.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  38 passed (38)
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- `exec.workdir` with leading `~`/`~/` now expands to home directory for local gateway execution

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- Tilde expansion only affects gateway-local paths; uses `expandHomePrefix()` from the existing home-dir helper

What is the highest-risk area?
- Edge case where homedir() returns an unexpected path

How is that risk mitigated?
- If the expanded path does not exist, the existing fallback mechanism still applies
- The fix is scoped to gateway execution only; node and sandbox workdir behavior is unchanged
- Regression test proves node `~/...` values are forwarded verbatim

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: feat(ios): modernize the app with iOS 26 Liquid Glass
target.body: Related: #98317 ## What Problem This Solves The iOS app mixed custom card glass, gradients, compact controls, and inconsistent corner geometry across its primary tabs. That weakened hierarchy on iOS 26, made content compete with system chrome, and left several navigation-adjacent controls looking older than the platform around them. ## Why This Change Was Made This modernizes the shared iOS design system around Apple's iOS 26 guidance: native Liquid Glass for interactive chrome, quiet semantic surfaces for content, shared concentric geometry, a minimizing tab bar, and one shared notice pattern. The deployment target remains iOS 18; every iOS 26 API is availability-gated with a functional bordered/material fallback. The design audit is captured in `apps/ios/DESIGN.md`, informed by Apple's [Adopting Liquid Glass](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/adopting-liquid-glass), [Applying Liquid Glass to custom views](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/applying-liquid-glass-to-custom-views), and [Build a SwiftUI app with the new design](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/323/) guidance. ## User Impact Control, Talk, Agent, Settings, transient notices, and shared content cards now present a more coherent native hierarchy. Primary actions and header controls adopt iOS 26 Liquid Glass, content cards remain calm and readable, the tab bar minimizes while scrolling, Agent no longer shows a duplicate navigation title, and earlier iOS versions retain equivalent actions and accessibility semantics. ## Evidence - iPhone 17 simulator, iOS 26.5, Xcode 26.6. - `RootTabsSourceGuardTests` + `SwiftUIRenderSmokeTests`: 43/43 passed after the final review fix. - `OpenClawSnapshotUITests.testConnectedGatewayTabs`: passed with five deterministic primary-tab captures. - `OpenClawSnapshotUITests.testLiveGatewayControlOverviewNavigation`: passed end to end against a real isolated OpenClaw Gateway; connected through setup, opened Control, navigated into Overview, and asserted `Back to Control` without a crash. - Production SwiftFormat lint and `git diff --check`: passed. - Structured Codex autoreview: one accepted semantic-color finding fixed; final pass clean with no actionable findings (0.93 confidence). ### Before and after Matched iPhone 17 / iOS 26.5 captures follow below. Both sides use the same deterministic connected fixture and light appearance. | Screen | Before | After | |---|---|---| | Control | | | | Chat | | | | Talk | | | | Agent | | | | Settings | | | ### Live Gateway proof These captures come from the real Gateway test rather than the deterministic fixture: first the connected Control screen, then the opened Overview destination. | Connected Control | Overview opened | |---|---| | | |

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: Open, Download, and Copy.
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably.
- Retries managed-image preview/action fetches with ordered Control UI auth candidates after `401/403`, so stale read-token credentials do not block owner-capable credentials.
- Binds requester-session headers to authenticated owner/admin context before serving managed image bytes.
- Renders generated image frames with stable rounded clipping, known-size geometry, and reserved pending-preview space.
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract.
- Restacked on current `main` and the clean #75727 head; the previous stale media/trust history is no longer in this PR head.
- Removed the public `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` config surface; Control UI thumbnails use the internal default.

## What Problem This Solves

Generated images were displayed without the full media affordances expected in chat. The implementation needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry, full-size action targets, and a safer managed-image auth path that does not strand a browser on stale credentials.

## Evidence

- Current head tested: `3442340b3200d251a8a8a60c7419e88ee1209066`.
- Behavior addressed: generated/managed chat images render as authenticated thumbnail previews; Open, Download, and Copy are visible browser actions; thumbnail and full-image action fetches retry owner-capable credentials after stale `401/403` credentials; the public thumbnail-size config knob is gone.
- Real environment tested: exact-head local Linux checkout in a real Chromium browser via Vite using the Control UI source renderer, plus focused gateway/UI/config shards and oxlint.

Commands run after the current fix/proof:

```bash
git diff --check

```

Live Chromium image-action evidence:

- `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` verifies ordered unique auth candidates.

## Security Impact

target.title: fix(cron): add Authorization header to provider preflight probe
target.body:
## Summary

Replaced the hand-rolled Authorization header logic in the cron preflight probe with the shared `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` from `provider-request-config.ts`. This ensures the preflight and normal model request paths build identical auth headers for the same config.

## Changes

### P1: Reuse shared auth normalization (ClawSweeper finding)

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Replaced the manual `if (params.apiKey) { headers["Authorization"] = ... }` / custom-header construction with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` via `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest()`
- Auth is now resolved through the same code path as normal model requests — any future changes to auth formatting apply automatically
- Full credential chain preserved: `request.auth` override → `resolveApiKeyForProvider` fallback → no auth
- Also ensures `request.headers` are always passed through (even without `request.auth`), fixing a pre-existing gap where provider-level and request-level headers were silently dropped

### P0: Error redaction at cache-write time

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Applied `formatErrorMessage()` to errors before storing in `preflightCache`, preventing unredacted error objects from remaining in heap between cache write and read

### P0: Header prefix trimming consistency

- Prefix is now trimmed via shared `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest`, matching the behavior of `resolveAuthOverride` in the normal request path

### P2: Cross-path auth header parity tests

- **model-preflight.runtime.test.ts**: Added 4 parity tests (P0) verifying preflight and normal path produce identical auth headers for the same config
- Added 4 edge case tests (P1): empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker filtering
- Total: 31 tests pass

## Files Changed

| File | Δ |
|------|---|
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.ts` | +140/-40 |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts` | +445/-3 |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron job preflight was building custom auth headers (`request.auth.mode === "header"`) with hand-rolled logic that missed prefix trimming, header collision cleanup, and `request.headers` merging — inconsistent with the shared auth normalization used by normal model requests.

The fix replaces the entire preflight auth resolution with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()`, the same shared function used by the normal model request path. This ensures:
- Authorization header and custom-header auth are built identically in both paths
- Prefix trimming and header collision cleanup apply automatically
- `request.headers` are always forwarded (even without `request.auth`)

**Real environment tested:**
- **OS:** Ubuntu 24.04 (Oracle VPS)
- **Runtime:** Node.js v24.14.0
- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.5.18 (npm install)
- **Provider:** LiteLLM proxy at http://127.0.0.1:4000
- **Auth method:** LITELLM_API_KEY environment variable

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (original base auth case):**
```bash
node -e "const m = require('./dist/index.cjs'); m.probeCronModelProvider({ provider: 'litellm', model: 'gpt-4o' }).then(r => console.log(JSON.stringify(r)))"
```

**Evidence after fix:** LiteLLM proxy logs before (top) and after (bottom) the patch show GET /models now gets 200 OK instead of 401:

![Before/after terminal comparison](https://github.com/samson1357924/openclaw/releases/download/evd-pr83648/evidence-pr-img.jpg)

Before — no auth header, cron preflight triggers 401:
```
LiteLLM Proxy:ERROR - Exception occured - No api key passed in.
```

After — auth header sent, preflight succeeds:
```
127.0.0.1:45914 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

The comprehensive auth normalization refactoring (prefix trim, header merging, parity with normal path) is covered by the test suite below.

**Additional verification (unit test suite):**
```
 ✓ src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts (31 tests) 575ms
 ✓ src/agents/provider-request-config.test.ts (30 tests) 62ms
All 61 tests pass across both files. No regressions.

```
Coverage includes:
- 4 cross-path auth header parity tests (preflight headers match normal path headers)
- 4 edge case tests (empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker)
- Existing bearer auth, header mode, and fallback tests

**Observed result after fix:**
- Live LiteLLM probe: GET /models changed from 401 Unauthorized to 200 OK
- Unit tests: 31/31 pass (preflight), 30/30 pass (regression)
- Code review: no Critical or Important issues found across all 5 dimensions
- Auth delegation is correct with proper fallback semantics and edge case handling

**What was not tested:**
- Integration test with actual local provider endpoint (preflight tests mock `fetchWithSsrFGuard`)
- `prepareProviderRuntimeAuth` plugin auth exchange path (documented gap — preflight intentionally skips this for local provider probes)
- Provider-level headers (`providerConfig.headers`) — these require SecretInput resolution not available at preflight's raw config stage

target.title: fix(gateway): avoid duplicate launchd restart bootstrap
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw gateway restart` can create a second gateway on macOS installs where the gateway is managed by a user LaunchAgent with `RunAtLoad`/`KeepAlive`.

The failure mode is easiest to hit from non-Aqua contexts, for example SSH or another background shell on a Mac that also has a console/auto-login user session. The old restart path built the launchd target from the caller UID (`gui/${process.getuid()}`), then ran `launchctl kickstart -k`. If that failed, it fell back to `launchctl bootstrap` for that caller domain.

On multi-GUI-session machines, the running KeepAlive job may actually live in the console user domain, such as `gui/501`. Bootstrapping into a different or racing domain can leave two launchd supervisors alive. The symptom is EADDRINUSE churn around the gateway port, gateway lock contention, and services such as the iMessage host fighting between gateway processes.

## Repro Shape

1. macOS gateway installed as a user LaunchAgent with `RunAtLoad` and `KeepAlive`.
2. The active gateway is supervised in the console/auto-login GUI domain.
3. Invoke `openclaw gateway restart` from SSH or a background/non-Aqua context whose `process.getuid()` target is not the loaded job domain, or while launchd is already relaunching the job.
4. The old path can fall through from failed `kickstart -k` to `bootstrap`, creating a second loaded copy instead of restarting the existing one in place.

## Fix

- Probe launchd with `launchctl print <domain>/<label>` before taking restart action.
- Prefer the actual loaded domain among explicit env, console user, caller UID, and the existing `gui/501` fallback.
- Apply the loaded-domain probe to both restart surfaces that can touch launchd: the external helper in `src/infra/restart.ts` and the actual CLI service restart owner path in `src/daemon/launchd.ts`.
- If the job is already loaded, use `launchctl kickstart -k` and never bootstrap.
- Bootstrap only after `launchctl print` confirms the job is not loaded.
- If bootstrap races with another loader and reports already loaded, retry with `kickstart -k` rather than bootstrapping again.
- Add a post-restart listener-count check on the external helper so duplicate gateway listeners are surfaced as a failed restart instead of triggering another fallback.

This intentionally does not change the internal self-restart path in `process-respawn.ts`; launchd-supervised gateways still exit and let KeepAlive relaunch them there. The in-service LaunchAgent restart path still schedules the existing detached handoff before doing direct launchctl work in-process.

## Evidence

- External helper regression coverage models a loaded launchd job and asserts `triggerOpenClawRestart` runs `launchctl print gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway` followed by `launchctl kickstart -k gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway`, with no `bootstrap` call.
- CLI owner-path regression coverage now models `openclaw gateway restart` resolving a loaded console LaunchAgent after a caller-domain miss: `launchctl print gui/777/ai.openclaw.gateway`, then `launchctl print gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway`, then `launchctl enable gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway`, then `launchctl kickstart -k gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway`, with no `bootstrap` or `bootout` call.
- The genuinely-unloaded path remains covered separately, so `bootstrap` is still allowed only after `launchctl print` reports not loaded.
- The already-loaded bootstrap race is covered: status `37` now retries with `kickstart -k` and does not attempt a second bootstrap.
- Duplicate post-restart listeners are covered on the external helper: two gateway listener PIDs make the restart result fail with a duplicate-listener detail instead of falling through to another launchd action.
- No live production `openclaw gateway restart` was run while validating this fix, to avoid triggering the duplicate-process bug on the real gateway.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/restart.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service.test.ts`

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not support Telegram Guest Mode, so users could not summon a Telegram bot for a one-off answer from supported chats unless the bot was already participating through normal Telegram message handling. - Why it matters: Guest Mode lets an allowed Telegram user ask the bot for a visible, one-shot answer in a external group or normal 1:1 private chats between users, without turning that chat into a normal OpenClaw conversation. - What changed: Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled explicitly. Allowed guest requests are routed through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserve sender metadata, inspect supported current/replied Telegram media when Telegram provides file references, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, and deliver exactly one visible text response. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode is disabled by default and does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules while preserving configured account/channel agent routing. Telegram Guest answers are text/article-only: the Bot API answerGuestQuery endpoint does not accept local file uploads, so generated or local file outputs are described rather than attached in Guest Mode. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from supported Telegram chats, including groups where the bot is not a member and 1:1 private chats with other users, when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true` is configured. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Set `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Built Control UI assets. 5. Restarted the local gateway. 6. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an (external group where the bot was not a member, or a private chat with another user). (`@openclawbot what time is it? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 7. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 8. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 9. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, cross-platform release-package install proof, and cached file_id media-output delivery for Guest Mode. Direct local file upload in answerGuestQuery is not in scope because Telegram does not expose an upload parameter for this endpoint. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, preserves configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. Guest updates are only requested when Guest Mode is enabled, and guest update queueing is keyed by source chat + sender instead of the normal group lane. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled`. - omitted / `false`: ignore guest queries and do not request `guest_message` updates from Telegram. - `true`: request and handle Telegram guest queries. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. It intentionally does not apply per-group allowlists or normal group conversation bindings because the bot is not a member of the source group in the guest interaction. Guest Mode can inspect supported media included in the guest message or replied message when Telegram file references are available, but generated/local media outputs are not uploaded through answerGuestQuery; the agent should explain that limitation in the one visible text answer. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [Telegram guest_message] -> [not in allowed updates / no guest delivery path] -> [no visible answer] After: [Telegram guest_message] -> [account-level allowlist] -> [isolated direct-style guest session] -> [agent turn] -> [answerGuestQuery] -> [one visible Telegram answer] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No. - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) Yes. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No. - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a Telegram guest-response capability using Telegram Bot API `answerGuestQuery`. Guest access is gated by the existing Telegram account-level `allowFrom` list, and OpenClaw only requests `guest_message` updates when Guest Mode is explicitly enabled. Guest turns use isolated direct-style session keys, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, skip group conversation bindings, do not receive source chat history, and deliver only one visible response. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu Linux. - Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway. - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex, GPT-5.5. - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram. - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel configured with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`; bot token and account details redacted. ### Steps 1. Enable guest mode for the bot using the telegram BotFather. 2. Configure `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Build OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Build Control UI assets. 5. Restart the local gateway. 6. Send a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot is not a member (or a private chat with another user). 7. Confirm that Telegram shows one visible bot answer. 8. Send a guest query from a user that is not allowed by account-level allowFrom. 9. Send normal messages in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Expected - Allowed guest queries create an isolated guest session and produce one visible Telegram answer. - Unauthorized guest senders are blocked. - Normal Telegram group handling still works in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Actual - Allowed guest queries produced one visible answer. - Unauthorized guest senders were blocked. - Existing normal Telegram group handling still worked. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [X] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live Telegram Guest Mode round trip from a group where the bot was not a member. - Normal Telegram private chats with the bot. - Normal Telegram private chats with other users using the Guest Mode work. - Unauthorized guest sender blocked by account-level allowlist. - Normal Telegram groups where the bot is already a member still handled messages normally. - Control UI assets load after pnpm ui:build. - Local retest after the account-bound guest routing fix. - Edge cases checked: - Unauthorized guest sender fallback. - Guest isolation from normal group bindings. (Needs to remove bot in between from the group) - What you did not verify: - Bot-to-bot mode. - Long-running multi-account (>3) production soak. - Cross-platform release-package install proof. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. Local Codex review was run during the beta-branch iteration and again after moving the patch onto current `origin/main`. Actionable findings from the earlier rounds were addressed before opening the PR. The final main-based Codex review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes. - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Yes. - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Telegram Guest Mode supports only one visible answer for this interaction shape. - Mitigation: Guest delivery sends only the first final answer through answerGuestQuery. - Risk: Unauthorized users could trigger guest turns (new ingress style). - Mitigation: Guest authorization uses the Telegram account-level allowFrom list, matching the Telegram DM access boundary. ~Horo
