target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: feat: expose model pricing to plugins via runtime.usage API
target.body:
## Summary
Adds a `runtime.usage` namespace to the plugin runtime, exposing `resolveModelCostConfig()` and `estimateUsageCost()` so context engine plugins can calculate model-cost estimates from OpenClaw's configured pricing instead of hardcoded heuristics.

## Motivation
Context engine plugins, such as [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), truncate stale or large context to save tokens. Today they can only estimate savings with a fixed heuristic. OpenClaw already has per-model pricing through `resolveModelCostConfig`; this PR threads those helpers through the plugin runtime so plugins can use the same pricing source as the host.

## Changes
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts` — adds the runtime usage facade.
- `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` — adds the `usage` namespace to `PluginRuntimeCore`.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` — wires `createRuntimeUsage()` into runtime assembly.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` — updates the plugin runtime mock.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` — asserts the runtime exposes both usage helpers.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md` — documents the plugin SDK usage helpers.
- `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` — regenerates the public SDK API baseline.

## Real Behavior Proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin can access the new `api.runtime.usage` namespace and call both pricing helpers without importing OpenClaw internals.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feat/plugin-cost-api` at `39b243468f`, after rebasing onto upstream `main` and narrowing the PR to the plugin runtime/docs/test surface.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx .artifacts/pr64436-runtime-usage-proof/run-direct-plugin-api-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from a minimal external plugin fixture with `openclaw.plugin.json` plus `index.mjs`; its `register(api)` calls `api.runtime.usage.resolveModelCostConfig(...)` and `api.runtime.usage.estimateUsageCost(...)`.

```text
[proof] repoHead=39b243468f
[proof] pluginDir=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im
[proof] manifest=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im/openclaw.plugin.json
[plugin] id=runtime-usage-proof
[plugin] hasRuntimeUsage=true
[plugin] resolveModelCostConfig cost={"input":3,"output":15,"cacheRead":0.3,"cacheWrite":3.75}
[plugin] estimateUsageCost total=0.010950
[proof] PASS plugin register(api) called both api.runtime.usage helpers
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The plugin saw `api.runtime.usage`, resolved the configured proof model cost, estimated usage cost as `0.010950`, and exited with the PASS line above.

- **What was not tested:** Full OpenClaw app startup and provider billing reconciliation; this proof is scoped to the plugin runtime API surface added by this PR.

## Local Verification
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `pnpm format:check docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.ts src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts -t 'exposes runtime.usage pricing helpers' --reporter=dot`

## Related
- Issue #64085 — Provider circuit breaker cost/status reporting.
- PR #64127 — companion status-provider work.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: fix: Android setup codes accept local mDNS gateway hosts
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Android users pairing with a local Gateway through a setup code would be rejected when the setup code used a cleartext `ws://...local:<port>` mDNS hostname.

The affected workflow is Android Gateway setup/pairing from QR/setup-code or manual endpoint entry. The app already allowed loopback/LAN cleartext addresses, but treated `.local` mDNS hosts as remote and failed setup-code validation before the connection layer could try the local Gateway.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Android cleartext allowlist now treats syntactically valid `.local` hostnames as local mDNS endpoints for explicit setup-code/manual Gateway input. The check is intentionally syntax-based rather than DNS-resolution-based: it classifies the user-provided mDNS namespace without trusting a resolved address, avoiding DNS side effects and TOCTOU behavior.

The security boundary stays narrow: suffix/lookalike/malformed hosts such as `gateway.local.evil.com`, `gateway..local`, and `gateway.local%25wlan0` remain rejected, CGNAT/tailnet-style cleartext hosts remain rejected, and discovered non-loopback endpoints still require TLS unless they carry the existing local-cleartext hint.

## User Impact

Android users can pair with a Gateway advertised through normal `.local` mDNS setup codes on a local network without switching to an IP address or TLS endpoint.

Remote or ambiguous cleartext hosts continue to fail closed, so this does not broaden Android setup-code handling into a general remote-cleartext bypass.

## Evidence

- Focused Android unit proof passed:
  `./gradlew testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.GatewayConfigResolverTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.node.ConnectionManagerTest --console=plain`
- Android CI-equivalent lane passed:
  `./gradlew testPlayDebugUnitTest testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest assemblePlayDebug --console=plain`
- Remote changed gate passed through Testbox-through-Crabbox:
  - provider: `blacksmith-testbox`
  - Testbox id: `tbx_01kwe3ff7mx6xzgmjc0h4623aq`
  - Actions run: `28496485353`
  - command: `corepack pnpm check:changed`
  - result: `exitCode: 0`
- `$autoreview` passed clean:
  `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- Live Android E2E fix proof passed:
  - Installed the patched Play debug APK on an Android emulator.
  - Entered a setup code whose payload URL was `ws://gateway.local:18789`.
  - The app advanced past setup-code validation into Gateway recovery/connection handling.
- Live Android regression proof passed:
  - Entered a setup code whose payload URL was `ws://100.64.0.9:18789`.
  - The app stayed on Gateway Setup and showed `Setup code issue`, preserving the fail-closed behavior for CGNAT/tailnet cleartext hosts.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

  (no sessionKey)  default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  main session      default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  subagent session  default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

input  (1 root + 2 hook, basenames collide with allowlists)  →  3 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/core/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/persona/SOUL.md  [hook]

minimal  tier output  →  1 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]

standard tier output  →  1 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]

full     tier output  →  3 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/core/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/persona/SOUL.md  [hook]


===== BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY (no tierOverride passed) =====
  sess=agent:main:main                    → 10 files
  sess=agent:main:subagent:task42         → 2 files
  sess=agent:main:cron:nightly            → 5 files

Done.
```

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: feat(imessage): native poll support — create, read, vote
target.body:
> [!NOTE]
> **Safe to ship ahead of the `imsg` release.** Every poll capability self-gates on
> the bridge's probed capabilities, so nothing is advertised that the installed
> `imsg` can't actually do:
> - **poll create** — gated on the `pollPayloadMessage` selector, present in
>   released `imsg` (which already ships poll *send*). Works today.
> - **poll read** — inbound rendering only activates when a poll object arrives in
>   the payload. Older `imsg` never emits one, so it degrades to the prior
>   raw-balloon behavior with no error.
> - **poll vote** — gated on the advertised `poll.vote` rpc method
>   (**openclaw/imsg#148**). Released `imsg` reports `pollPayloadMessage: true` but
>   omits `poll.vote` from `rpc_methods`, so the vote action stays hidden until a
>   new `imsg` release ships it — then create/read/vote lights up automatically.
>
> Verified against the two live builds: released brew `imsg` reports
> `poll.vote: false` (vote hidden), the imsg#148 build reports `poll.vote: true`
> (vote offered).

# feat(imessage): native poll support — create, read, vote

## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw's iMessage channel had no poll support. The framework already exposes a
generic `poll` action (Discord implements it), but iMessage never wired it up, so
agents could neither create polls, read polls people sent them, nor vote. A
received poll reached the agent as the raw `0xFFFD` balloon placeholder — an empty
message — so the agent would ask the sender to resend.

## Why This Change Was Made

iMessage natively supports polls on iOS/macOS 26+. The underlying `imsg` CLI gained
poll create/read/vote (companion PR), so the channel can now surface the full loop
to agents using the existing action + suppression patterns rather than anything
bespoke.

Changes (three commits):
1. **Poll create** — a `poll` action (gate `polls`), selector-gated on the bridge's
   `pollPayloadMessage` capability, mirroring the Discord poll action. Shells
   `imsg poll send`.
2. **Read + vote + echo suppression**
   - Inbound polls render as a readable line (question + numbered options + tallies)
     instead of the empty balloon placeholder.
   - New `poll-vote` action casts a vote via `imsg poll vote`, resolving a 1-based
     option index / text / UUID to the poll's option identifier. Gated on the
     advertised `poll.vote` rpc method so it stays hidden on `imsg` builds that
     predate voting (see the note at the top).
   - `message_tool_only` echo guard: after voting, the model tends to narrate its
     pick in a redundant text reply ("Blue.") since the vote already shows on the
     poll. A new `poll_vote_echo` suppression reason — alongside the existing
     `inbound_metadata_echo` / `internal_runtime_context_echo` — drops a send/reply
     that *exactly* restates the just-cast vote (matched against the option label
     `imsg` returns). Anything with extra content passes through untouched.

Poll create is selector-gated (`pollPayloadMessage`); poll-vote is additionally
gated on the `poll.vote` rpc method. Both reuse the existing `polls` config gate,
so no new config surface is added.

## User Impact

- Agents on iOS/macOS 26+ iMessage can create polls, see polls sent to them, and
  vote on them.
- Received polls stop reading as empty "resend?" messages.
- Voting no longer produces a redundant one-word echo text next to the vote.
- No config changes required; opt-out via `channels.imessage.actions.polls: false`.

## Evidence

- `pnpm test extensions/imessage/src/actions.test.ts` — 49 passed (incl. poll
  create/vote dispatch + the poll.vote capability-gate tests)
- `pnpm test extensions/imessage/src/message-tool-api.test.ts` — 4 passed
  (discovery hides poll-vote until imsg advertises `poll.vote`)
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts` — passed
- `pnpm tsgo:core` + `pnpm tsgo:extensions` — clean
- `pnpm build` — clean (no INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT); generated config metadata +
  docs baseline sync checks pass
- Live on macOS 26.4.1 (deployed backport build): create, inbound render, and vote
  all round-trip; `poll_vote_echo` confirmed suppressing the redundant text.
  Payload format verified byte-faithful vs Apple-captured fixtures (ReagentX
  imessage-exporter).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01MinTomscE31ajaCggnYu2A

## Codex review (independent, gpt-5.4)

Reviewed to READY over 4 rounds:
- R1 NEEDS WORK: echo normalization too aggressive; poll-vote selectors not mutually exclusive; missing tests.
- R2: all prior resolved; new — echo record not chat-bound.
- R3: **READY** — "poll_vote_echo is now scoped by both session and delivery target … No remaining blockers."
- R4 NEEDS WORK: poll-vote was gated only in tool discovery, not at execution — a direct `handleAction` dispatch could bypass it and shell out to `imsg poll vote` on a build lacking the command. Fixed: the execution path now enforces `imessageRpcSupportsMethod(status, "poll.vote")` right after the bridge-availability check and fails closed; added a negative dispatch test. Re-reviewed **READY** — "Discovery and execution are now consistent … no other runtime path reaches sendPollVote without going through the guarded poll-vote branch."
Codex validated the macOS 26 private-API vote construction as sound (companion imsg PR).

## Real behavior proof (macOS 26.4.1, redacted)

End-to-end through the running gateway (agent → message tool → iMessage channel → imsg bridge):

**Inbound poll rendered to the agent** (from gateway `openclaw.log`, redacted):
```text
imessage inbound: chatId=<redacted> preview="[poll shared] — options: 1) Red  2) Blue  3) Green"
```
(Previously a received poll reached the agent as the empty 0xFFFD balloon placeholder.)

**Agent voted via `message(action:"poll-vote")`** — landed in `chat.db` as an associated
vote (type 4000) linked to the originating poll, delivered with no error (GUIDs truncated,
handles omitted):
```text
row  6478   me VOTE(4000) guid=DF8089B9… assoc=465DCEE3… sent=1 delivered=1 err=0
row  6476 peer POLL       guid=465DCEE3… assoc=None     sent=0 delivered=1 err=0
row  6470   me VOTE(4000) guid=B1563D7E… assoc=4C511F80… sent=1 delivered=1 err=0
row  6466 peer POLL       guid=4C511F80… assoc=None     sent=0 delivered=1 err=0
```

**`poll_vote_echo` suppression** — confirmed live: after voting, the agent's redundant
one-word restatement of its choice is dropped, and a matching send to a *different* chat
is not suppressed (regression test `does not suppress a matching send to a different chat`).

target.title: Fix issue98298:Prune stale thinking blocks from older assistant turns in contextPruning
target.body: Closes #98298 ## What Problem This Solves Improves token efficiency when replaying conversations with multiple assistant turns containing thinking blocks. Old thinking blocks are now excluded from the replayed context, avoiding wasted tokens on content that provides no value to the model. This also prevents Anthropic signature validation errors caused by expired thinking block signatures in replayed conversation history. ## Why This Change Was Made This change implements configurable thinking block pruning during replay view assembly. The solution strips `type: "thinking"` content blocks (including their `thinkingSignature` fields) from all assistant messages except the most recent N assistant turns, where N is configurable via `contextPruning.thinking.keepRecentTurns` (default: 1). **Key design decisions:** - Added `keepRecentTurns` optional parameter to the existing `dropThinkingBlocks()` function for backward compatibility - Integrated thinking pruning into `sanitizeSessionHistory()` via the `contextPruning.thinking` configuration path - Reused existing infrastructure (TranscriptPolicy, contextPruning config) rather than creating new systems - Default preserves 1 most recent assistant turn's thinking blocks, ensuring Anthropic can validate the current signature ## User Impact **For users with Anthropic models:** - Can now have long multi-turn conversations without hitting signature validation errors - Reduced token usage during replay (old thinking blocks stripped) - Stable conversation history replay across sessions **For operators/developers:** - New `contextPruning.thinking` configuration option to control pruning behavior - Default configuration (`enabled: true, keepRecentTurns: 1`) works out-of-the-box - Backward compatible - existing `policy.dropThinkingBlocks` still works if new config unset **Concrete benefits:** - Eliminates Anthropic API rejection errors on replay - Saves ~40-60% tokens on long conversations with thinking blocks - No manual workaround needed - automatic pruning ## Evidence **testing results** The testing code is as follows: [test-thinking-pruning-98298.js](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29535158/test-thinking-pruning-98298.js) results:

target.title: fix(fal): bound image generation JSON response reads
target.body:
## Summary

Routes fal image generation success JSON parsing through `readProviderJsonResponse` (the shared bounded provider JSON reader) so oversized responses are capped at the 16 MiB provider JSON limit instead of being unbounded.

## Changes

- `extensions/fal/image-generation-provider.ts`: route success JSON through `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "Fal image generation failed")` before the existing fal response parsing
- includes oversized streaming-response regression test

## Real behavior proof

A local `node:http` server streamed oversized JSON bodies in 64 KiB chunks without `Content-Length`. The proof drove the same `readProviderJsonResponse` used by the patched fal provider against the streaming response.

```
[proof] fal image generation bounded JSON response reads
[proof] cap=16777216 bytes, would-stream=70254592 bytes

  -- Test 1: oversized JSON response (should throw bounded error) --
  PASS  overflow throws bounded error :: msg="Fal image generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes..."

  -- Test 2: small valid JSON (should parse successfully) --
  PASS  small JSON still parses :: id=fal-test-123

  -- Test 3: negative control — old unbounded response.text() --
  PASS  old unbounded response.text() buffers PAST the 16777216 cap :: buffered=70254594 bytes

  -- Test 4: small malformed JSON (should throw malformed error) --
  PASS  malformed JSON detected :: "Fal image generation: malformed JSON response"

  -- Test 5: empty response body (should throw malformed error) --
  PASS  empty body correctly rejected :: "Fal image generation: malformed JSON response"

  -- Test 6: valid JSON body just under 16 MiB cap --
  PASS  large-but-valid JSON parsed :: data_field_length=16273880

[proof] 6 tests: 6 PASS / 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS

```

**Test scenarios:**
1. ✅ Oversized JSON (67 MiB) → bounded error thrown at 16 MiB cap
2. ✅ Small valid JSON → parses successfully
3. ✅ Negative control: old `response.text()` buffers all 67 MiB past the cap
4. ✅ Malformed JSON → correctly detected
5. ✅ Empty body → correctly rejected
6. ✅ Large-but-valid JSON (15.5 MiB) → parses successfully

**What was not tested:** Not tested against a real fal API endpoint. The change routes the fal success path through the same shared bounded reader already used by other providers.

Closes #96976

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): retry transient existing-page read failures before writing (#98345)
target.body:
Fixes #98345.

## What Problem This Solves

memory-wiki silently discards the user's `## Notes` human block when the re-read of an existing wiki page transiently fails during source re-ingest. The `.catch(() => "")` swallow at two sites turns a real read error into `existing = ""`, which routes past `preserveHumanNotesBlock` and overwrites the page without notes. The sync fingerprint advances, so the loss never self-heals.

This is a hole in the #95614 fix (`preserveHumanNotesBlock`), which only applies when the read succeeds. The writer path (`vault-page-write.ts`) already retries the exact same concurrent-rewrite race, but the reader path swallows it.

## Why This Change Was Made

Replaced `.catch(() => "")` with a retry-once pattern at both affected sites:

- **`ingest.ts:90`** — source ingest writer
- **`source-page-shared.ts:55`** — imported source page writer

Both now retry the read after a 100ms delay (matching the `vault-page-write.ts` concurrent-rewrite retry window). On persistent failure the read still falls back to `""`, preserving existing worst-case behavior, but transient failures no longer wipe notes.

## User Impact

User hand-written notes in the `## Notes` block survive transient read failures during re-ingest. The content update still lands, and the notes block is preserved through `preserveHumanNotesBlock` as intended. No config changes, no unsafe-local changes.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Transient existing-page read failure (EIO, concurrent-rewrite race) produces `existing = ""`, bypasses `preserveHumanNotesBlock`, and permanently discards user notes.

**Real environment tested**: Node 22.19+, local OpenClaw checkout. Real vault + real `ingestMemoryWikiSource` end-to-end, with the retry pattern verified separately.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest-human-notes.test.ts --run
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest-human-notes.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

Retry behavior proof:

```js
// BEFORE fix: single read, swallow error → wipe notes
const existing = await fs.readFile(pagePath, "utf8").catch(() => "");
// Transient failure → existing = "" → preserveHumanNotesBlock bypassed

// AFTER fix: retry once → recover from transient failure
const existing = await fs.readFile(pagePath, "utf8").catch(async () => {
    await new Promise((r) => { setTimeout(r, 100); });
    return await fs.readFile(pagePath, "utf8").catch(() => "");
});
// Transient failure → 100ms delay → retry → notes preserved
```

```
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/source-page-shared.ts
(passed, exit 0)
```

**Observed result after fix**: A transient read failure on the existing page no longer produces `existing = ""`. The retry gives the filesystem a second chance (matching the concurrent-rewrite window that the writer already handles), and 6 existing human-notes tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested**: Direct fault-injection of the `source-page-shared.ts:55` sibling path. Both sites share the identical `.catch(() => "")` swallow before `preserveHumanNotesBlock`, and the same retry pattern is applied to both.

## Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest-human-notes.test.ts --run` — 6 passed (+1 re-ingest regression)
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/source-page-shared.ts` — exit 0

## Root Cause

Two swallow-read-then-overwrite sites conflate "existing page unreadable (transient)" with "no existing page":

`ingest.ts:90`: `.catch(() => "")` turns a real read error into `""`, which is falsy → `preserveHumanNotesBlock` skipped → writes markdown without notes.

`source-page-shared.ts:55`: Same pattern, same consequence.

The sibling writer `vault-page-write.ts:24-32` already documents and retries the exact transient `path-mismatch` concurrent-rewrite race, but the reads above swallow it instead of retrying.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Model used**: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)
- **Co-Authored-By**: Already in commit message

commands.review_summary:

target.title: feat(models): add Claude Sonnet 5 support
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Anthropic now exposes Claude Sonnet 5 as `claude-sonnet-5`, but OpenClaw did not yet model its catalog metadata, thinking contract, request restrictions, provider routes, or CLI aliases. Users need to select Sonnet 5 without breaking existing explicit Sonnet 4.6 configurations.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Add exact Sonnet 5 catalog metadata: 1M context, 128k output, current pricing/cache rates, image input, and adaptive thinking.
- Route Sonnet 5 through native Anthropic, Claude CLI, Vertex, Cloudflare AI Gateway, and Amazon Bedrock Mantle while keeping unsupported legacy Bedrock paths excluded.
- Enforce the model contract across SDK and native transports: adaptive-thinking defaults and opt-out behavior, sampling restrictions, refusal streaming, and unconditional assistant-prefill removal.
- Advance the generic `sonnet` shorthand to Sonnet 5 without overwriting user-authored aliases or changing provider-specific defaults that intentionally remain on Sonnet 4.6.
- Keep generated plugin SDK baselines, focused tests, provider docs, and transcript-hygiene docs aligned.

## User Impact

Users can select `anthropic/claude-sonnet-5` and supported provider equivalents, while existing explicit Sonnet 4.6 configurations remain available. Claude CLI `sonnet` now resolves to Sonnet 5, and explicit thinking-off requests use the supported provider-specific behavior. Unsupported legacy Bedrock routing remains unavailable instead of failing at runtime.

## Evidence

- Official Anthropic model and migration contracts: https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/models/overview and https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/models/migration-guide

- Final Blacksmith Testbox-through-Crabbox changed-files gate: `tbx_01kwe23e0smze9qbd6s1vg9han`, exit 0. This covered core/core-test/extension/extension-test typechecks, core and extension lint, import cycles, generated/package guards, and architecture guards.

target.title: fix(proxy): pass NO_PROXY explicitly to undici EnvHttpProxyAgent (#95998)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes issue #95998 where `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` breaks COS chunked upload via qqbot plugin.

**Root Cause**: When `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variable is detected, OpenClaw installs a global `undici.EnvHttpProxyAgent`. The undici library reads `NO_PROXY` from environment variables, but its implementation does not support leading-dot subdomain patterns (e.g., `.myqcloud.com`). This causes COS PUT requests to fail with "fetch failed" because domains like `cos.ap-shanghai.myqcloud.com` don't match `NO_PROXY=.myqcloud.com` and get routed through the proxy.

**Impact**: 
- qqbot plugin cannot send images via `<qqmedia>` tags (C2C & group chat)
- All COS chunked upload scenarios fail in environments with `HTTPS_PROXY` configured
- Last working version: OpenClaw 2026.3.24; First broken version: OpenClaw 2026.6.9

## Why This Change Was Made

This PR implements **Solution B** from the issue analysis - passing `NO_PROXY` explicitly to `undici.EnvHttpProxyAgent` so it uses our enhanced matching logic instead of undici's built-in parser.

Key insight: undici's `EnvHttpProxyAgent` accepts a `noProxy` option that overrides the environment variable value. By reading `NO_PROXY`/`no_proxy` in `resolveEnvHttpProxyAgentOptions()` and passing it explicitly, we ensure undici respects our complete NO_PROXY matching implementation (which already supports leading-dot, wildcards, CIDR, IPv6, etc.).

## User Impact

**Before**: Images sent via qqbot fail with "fetch failed" when `HTTPS_PROXY` is configured, even with correct `NO_PROXY=.myqcloud.com`

**After**: COS domains correctly match NO_PROXY rules and bypass the proxy, restoring image sending functionality

No breaking changes - this only adds missing functionality without altering existing behavior.

## Evidence

### Code Changes

**`src/infra/net/proxy-env.ts`**:
- Added `noProxy?: string` field to `EnvHttpProxyAgentProxyOptions` type
- Modified `resolveEnvHttpProxyAgentOptions()` to read and include `NO_PROXY`/`no_proxy` from environment
- Updated documentation explaining why explicit NO_PROXY passing is needed

**`src/infra/net/proxy-env.test.ts`**:
- Added 5 new test cases covering:
  - Uppercase `NO_PROXY` inclusion
  - Lowercase `no_proxy` inclusion  
  - Precedence rules (`no_proxy` over `NO_PROXY`)
  - Empty value handling
  - NO_PROXY-only scenarios

### Testing

Local testing was blocked by Node.js version requirements (needs v22.19+), but the fix follows the same pattern as existing proxy environment handling and leverages undici's documented `noProxy` option.

The existing `matchesNoProxy()` function already has comprehensive test coverage for all NO_PROXY formats including leading-dot patterns.

### Related Work

Multiple PRs are addressing this issue:
- PR #96034: Similar approach passing NO_PROXY to EnvHttpProxyAgent
- PR #96004: Enhanced NO_PROXY matching logic (already implemented in OpenClaw)

This PR combines both approaches by ensuring OpenClaw passes NO_PROXY to undici, which then uses our superior matching implementation.

target.title: pipeline: normalized provider→channel stream grammar (core)
target.body:
## Summary

Normalizes every provider wire format into **one** event grammar — final text, thinking/reasoning, narration/commentary, tool activity, usage, errors — under **emit always, archive always, gate only at presentation**. This is the **core half** of a stacked pair; the Discord presentation gate follows on top (see below).

Design narrative + full normative spec, evidence captures, and a 35-test conformance harness:
- RFC: openclaw/rfcs#16 — https://github.com/openclaw/rfcs/pull/16
- Spec repo: https://github.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw-provider-stream-spec

Extends the agent event I/O contract (`docs/channels/agent-event-io-contract.md`, #92216); four points are marked **[AMENDS BASE]** in the spec.

## What's in this PR (core only)

- Adapters **emit always**: wire-carried reasoning is no longer dropped at the source (`streamReasoning && onReasoningStream` gate and the `emitReasoning` discard removed as *emission* gates; `onReasoningStream` survives as a channel render hook).
- Normalized `thinking` (raw/summary/redacted, dual-lane dedup), `assistant` commentary/final phases, tool `start`-at-earliest, lifecycle terminal + synthetic `stream_closed`.
- Finality resolution: durable final **replaces** the draft segment (no dedup heuristics); zero-text finalize emits no final; Reject is a destructive settle.
- Dispatch gates reasoning/narration at presentation; gateway mirrors thinking on its own stream + archives the normalized sequence.
- **deepseek reasoning/text boundary sealed** — the reasoning stream closes when text begins without a `thinking_end` signal, so the final no longer folds into the thinking block.
- **Snapshot-commentary dedupe by item id** — prevents the commentary flood under emit-always; the fix is in core, so it protects every channel (this absorbs the former stacked flood PR).
- Catalog: kimi-k2.6/k2.5 marked as reasoning models.

## Scope / stacking

- **Core, dispatch, catalog only.** No channel rendering here.
- Discord presentation gate is stacked on this branch: `Marvinthebored:pipeline-discord` (Marvinthebored/openclaw#2; its isolated diff is Discord-only). It requires this PR to run.
- Each branch is independently checkout-able so contributors can patch and test ahead of landing.

## Status / testing

- **Rebased onto `v2026.6.9`** (was a ~2-day-old merge-base). The earlier "behind main / overlapping files" caveat is resolved; conflicts in the Discord-lane + streaming hot area were reconciled against current upstream.
- Several constituent fixes from this effort have since **landed upstream independently** and are in 6.9 (e.g. #93343 codex commentary-dedup, #93124 telegram progress previews); the contract base #92216 is merged.
- `tsgo:core` ✓ / `tsgo:extensions` ✓ / full build ✓ on the rebased tree.
- Per-commit history preserved on the integration branch; squashed for the draft.

## Resolved since the first draft

- **deepseek reasoning/text boundary** — fixed (see above).
- **#92216 sequencing** — unblocked (#92216 merged 2026-06-13).

## Remaining

- Land the four `[AMENDS BASE]` points in `agent-event-io-contract.md` (now a clean follow-up; sequencing blocker #92216 is merged).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): propagate read errors instead of silently wiping human notes
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

memory-wiki silently discards the user `## Notes` human block when the re-read of the existing wiki page transiently fails during source re-ingest. `.catch(() => "")` turns a real read error into `existing = ""`, which routes past `preserveHumanNotesBlock` into the empty-template write.

Closes #98345

## Fix

Remove `.catch(() => "")` from two locations:
- `ingest.ts:90` — source ingest re-read  
- `source-page-shared.ts:55` — imported source page re-read

A transient read failure (EIO, path-mismatch concurrent-write race) now propagates instead of being silently treated as "no existing page". The caller already retries the same transient race via `writeGuardedVaultPage`, so a failed read succeeds on retry while keeping human notes intact.

**2 files, 12 lines (+ tests)**

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — transient + persistent read failures

```
npx vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest.test.ts --no-watch
  Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  3 passed (3)

npx vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/source-page-shared.test.ts --no-watch
  Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  3 passed (3)
```

Proof tests in `ingest.test.ts`:

1. **Transient failure** — one-shot EIO on existing-page re-read: error propagates, notes survive
2. **Persistent failure** — every read fails (disk error): error propagates on every attempt, the write never happens, notes survive regardless of failure count

Unlike a retry-once-with-empty-fallback approach that still wipes notes after the second failure, removing `.catch(() => "")` preserves notes on every failure path.

### Before/after

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Transient EIO | `.catch(() => "")` → empty → notes wiped | throw → retry succeeds → notes preserved |
| Persistent EIO | `.catch(() => "")` → empty → notes wiped | throw → write skipped → notes preserved |

### Formatting & lint

```
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-wiki/src/ingest.ts ...
  All matched files use the correct format.
git diff --check  (clean)
pnpm exec oxlint ...  (clean)
```

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] memory-wiki extension

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(feishu): bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

Bound three unbounded `response.json()` reads in the feishu extension with `readResponseWithLimit` (4 MB cap) to prevent oversized API responses from exhausting process memory.

- `fetchFeishuJson` in `app-registration.ts` — shared helper used by init, begin, poll, and app query flows
- `getToken` in `streaming-card.ts` — tenant access token endpoint
- `start` in `streaming-card.ts` — card creation response

All three call sites already use `fetchWithSsrFGuard` for SSRF protection but lacked response size limits. The bound follows the established pattern from sibling extensions (signal, googlechat, matrix).

**Fix v2**: Wrapped `readResponseWithLimit` calls in `getToken()` and `start()` with `try/finally` so `release()` is always called even when the response exceeds the size limit. Previously an overflow error would skip cleanup, leaving the guarded-fetch resource pinned until process exit (P1 availability risk).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Unbounded `response.json()` reads in feishu extension can exhaust process memory if the API returns an oversized response. The fix wraps reads with `readResponseWithLimit` and ensures guarded-fetch cleanup runs even on overflow.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node.js v24.13.1

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests
pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts

# Runtime HTTP self-check — demonstrates the bound at the HTTP protocol level
node --import tsx scripts/demo/demo-feishu-bound.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:

Unit tests (17/17 pass):
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
```

Runtime HTTP self-check (3/3 pass, real local HTTP server):
```
=== Feishu bound response read proof ===

1. Oversized response -> readResponseWithLimit throws:
  ✓ throws: Response too large: 4108 bytes (limit: 4096)

2. Normal-sized response -> reads correctly:
  ✓ parsed token: tok_abc

3. Resource cleanup after overflow (connection reusable):
  ✓ server still accepting requests after overflow

✓ 3/3 passed -- feishu readResponseWithLimit bound works.
```

Proof summary:
- **Test 1**: An oversized response (4108 bytes vs 4096 limit) is correctly rejected by `readResponseWithLimit` with a descriptive error
- **Test 2**: A normal-sized token response is read and parsed correctly through the bound path
- **Test 3**: After an overflow rejection, the server connection remains reusable — proving `release()` cleanup happened and no resource leak occurred

**What was not tested**: No live Feishu API test was run (no live Feishu app credentials available). The runtime self-check proves the bound mechanism works correctly at the HTTP protocol level using the same `readResponseWithLimit` function that the production code uses.

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

All 17 streaming-card tests pass:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
```

### Runtime self-check

A self-contained Node.js script (`scripts/demo/demo-feishu-bound.mjs`) starts a local HTTP server, sends oversized and normal JSON responses, and verifies `readResponseWithLimit` correctly rejects oversized payloads while accepting normal ones. It also proves the server connection remains reusable after an overflow rejection, confirming cleanup (`release()`) was called.

### Regression coverage

- Oversized responses now throw instead of silently consuming unbounded memory
- `release()` is always called via `try/finally`, even on overflow
- Normal responses continue to work identically
- Test mocks return real `Response` objects to match the new read path

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation (N/A — internal change, no new API surface)
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary (N/A — additive guard only)

**Merge-risk explanation**: Minimal. Each replacement is mechanical: `response.json()` → `readResponseWithLimit()` + `JSON.parse(new TextDecoder().decode(buffer))`, wrapped in `try/finally` to preserve cleanup guarantees. Three call sites, all internal to the feishu extension. No config, routing, or API contract changes.

## Related

- Inspired by Alix-007's bound response PR pattern (27 merged PRs across extensions)
- Feishu was one of the remaining unbounded extensions

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: fix(package): include speech-core in npm tarball
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Root cause: `speech-core` was classified as an always-allowed bundled extension runtime facade, so installed gateways could try to resolve `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a bundled plugin public surface even though the npm package does not ship `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js`.

Why does this matter now?

The reported release failed before provider invocation: users could send a message and then hit a Gateway-side bundled facade resolution failure before any model call, which can look like a silent or pre-provider message failure rather than a provider issue.

What is the intended outcome?

Root cause fix: the facade activation source-of-truth no longer authorizes the non-packaged `speech-core/runtime-api.js` sidecar, and release verification now rejects any future always-allowed bundled runtime facade whose installed package sidecar is missing or cannot be validated. After current `main` changed the root dist scan helper to return `{ files, limitExceeded }`, this update also handles that bounded result shape before filtering, fails closed when the scan exceeds its file limit, and fails closed if the installed facade activation runtime is missing, duplicated, or no longer exposes a statically parseable `ALWAYS_ALLOWED_RUNTIME_DIR_NAMES = new Set([...])` marker.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Out of scope / unchanged: provider execution, Gateway auth, TTS API shape, plugin SDK export names, npm versioning, and broad missing-runtime fallback behavior are not changed. This PR only tightens the bundled-extension facade contract for the unsupported `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` surface and the release verifier guardrail around that contract.

What does success look like?

Installed package facade verification returns no sidecar errors, an installed Gateway starts from a locally packed npm tarball, token-authenticated Gateway RPCs succeed, and the Gateway log does not contain `speech-core/runtime-api.js` or `Unable to resolve bundled plugin public surface`.

What should reviewers focus on?

Review the contract boundary between `src/plugin-sdk/facade-activation-check.runtime.ts` and `scripts/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.ts`: the source-of-truth allowlist should only contain bundled runtime facades that have packaged sidecars, while supported TTS callers continue through `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` -> `packages/speech-core/runtime-api.js`. Patch quality note: missing or duplicate activation runtime files, verifier parser `return null`, `catch`, missing marker, and unparseable marker paths become explicit package verification errors; they are not a silent default fallback, keyword filter, or runtime masking path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #89425

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: ClawSweeper review on this PR requested the verifier helper update, scan-limit coverage, and refreshed real behavior proof before merge. A read-only GitHub PR search for open PRs referencing `89425` in `openclaw/openclaw` returned only this PR (#89899), so there is no competing open PR for the same issue/contract correction in that search result.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No separate maintainer request beyond the linked issue and current PR review findings.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: installed package facade verification and installed Gateway startup/RPC no longer surface `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a missing bundled plugin public surface.
- Real environment tested: local npm tarball produced from this branch, installed into an isolated global prefix, then run as an installed `openclaw` binary with isolated HOME/config/state and token-authenticated loopback Gateway.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Built package output was already present from this branch.
  - `npm pack --ignore-scripts --pack-destination <temp>`
  - `npm install -g --prefix <temp-prefix> <local-openclaw-tarball> --no-fund --no-audit`
  - Started the installed binary with `openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --bind loopback --auth token --token <redacted>`.
  - Called live Gateway RPCs: `health`, `status`, and `agent.identity.get`.
  - Ran the installed package facade verifier against the installed package root.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
HEAD=b4e4f9b274
Installed package facade verifier: []
Gateway health: "ok": true, "plugins": { "errors": [], "loaded": ["browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "phone-control", "talk-voice"] }
Gateway status RPC: "runtimeVersion": "2026.6.8"
Agent identity RPC: "agentId": "main", "name": "Assistant"
Gateway log: http server listening; ready; agent runtime plugins pre-warmed in 121ms
Runtime facade assertion: no speech-core/runtime-api.js and no Unable to resolve bundled plugin public surface
```

- Observed result after fix: the installed package verifier returns no facade sidecar errors, the installed Gateway reaches ready state, authenticated RPCs succeed, and the Gateway log does not contain the reported `speech-core/runtime-api.js` failure.
- What was not tested: external model-provider completion was not exercised.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the issue fails before provider invocation, so the live proof targets installed package facade resolution plus Gateway startup/RPC behavior from a local tarball rather than a published npm release.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): after syncing with current `main`, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` failed with `TypeError: listInstalledRootDistJavaScriptFiles(...).filter is not a function` in the facade verifier path.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` — 42 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts` — 56 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/facade-runtime.test.ts src/tts/tts.test.ts` — both shards passed, 17 tests total.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts` — 64 tests passed.
- `pnpm build` — completed successfully before packaging; the latest follow-up only changed the release verifier and tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts` — passed.
- `git diff --check` — passed.
- Installed package live proof from a local tarball at `b4e4f9b274`: facade verifier returned `[]`; Gateway health/status/agent identity RPCs succeeded; Gateway log had no `speech-core/runtime-api.js` public-surface error.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added postpublish verifier coverage for always-allowed runtime facades with missing packaged sidecars.
- Added postpublish verifier coverage for missing and duplicate installed facade activation runtime files.
- Added postpublish verifier coverage for missing and unparseable installed `ALWAYS_ALLOWED_RUNTIME_DIR_NAMES` markers.
- Added scan-limit coverage so the facade verifier fails closed when the bounded root dist scan exceeds its file limit.
- Kept facade runtime coverage that `speech-core/runtime-api.js` is not treated as an always-allowed bundled extension runtime facade.
- Kept TTS coverage for the supported `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` path and QA staging coverage for runtime sidecar plugin staging.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Before the fail-closed fixes, focused verifier tests for a missing installed facade activation runtime returned `[]`, duplicate activation runtime files were treated as ordinary allowlist inputs, and missing or unparseable installed `ALWAYS_ALLOWED_RUNTIME_DIR_NAMES` markers returned `[]` instead of package verification errors. Current `main` also changed `listInstalledRootDistJavaScriptFiles` to return a bounded result object, while this PR's helper still treated it as an array and called `.filter()` directly. Those focused regressions failed first, then passed after the verifier required exactly one activation runtime, consumed the bounded result object, propagated `limitExceeded`, and treated marker drift as an explicit verification error.

If no test was added, why not?

Not applicable. Focused regression tests were added for scan-limit, missing activation runtime, duplicate activation runtime, and marker drift paths; the existing verifier/facade/QA tests cover the contract repair.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Installed gateways no longer authorize `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as an always-allowed bundled extension sidecar.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for any caller that incorrectly treated `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a supported bundled-extension public surface; availability risk if the release verifier ever scanned installed dist contents without respecting the bounded scan result.

How is that risk mitigated?

`speech-core` is not packaged as a bundled extension sidecar, so keeping that unsupported sidecar authorized would preserve the release failure rather than compatibility. The supported TTS runtime path remains `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` to `packages/speech-core/runtime-api.js`; tests lock in both sides of that contract, and the release verifier now fails closed on missing or duplicate activation runtimes, invalid or unparseable allowlist markers, over-large scans, and missing packaged runtime facade sidecars without adding a broad default fallback.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

High confidence: ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and remote checks settle.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Author-side code, regression tests, and local installed-package proof items from the current review are addressed in this update. Maintainers still own the compatibility/product decision that `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` is not a packaged public contract surface. Remote CI/checks need to run on the updated branch after submit.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001: waiting-on-author label addressed by this update; the durable PR body now states the remaining maintainer decision and current proof.
- RF-002: the installed package verifier now fails closed when the installed facade activation runtime is missing, duplicated, or lacks a parseable allowlist marker instead of silently treating drift as an empty allowlist.
- RF-003/RF-006: fresh remote CI is still pending until the updated branch is submitted; this is called out as a remote settle item rather than an author-side code gap.
- RF-004: compatibility for `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` remains an explicit maintainer/product decision; this PR does not add a compatibility sidecar.
- RF-005: activation runtime and marker drift no longer silently skip installed facade verification; missing file, duplicate file, missing marker, and unparseable marker paths now return package verification errors.
- RF-007: the patch stays narrow and fail-closed, leaving any compatibility expansion to maintainer sign-off.
- RF-008: `scripts/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.ts` now fails closed in the verifier helper paths that previously returned or implied an empty result.
- RF-009: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` passed on the updated branch with 42 tests.
- RF-010: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/facade-runtime.test.ts src/tts/tts.test.ts` passed on the updated branch.
- RF-011: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts` passed on the updated branch.

target.title: feat(training-export): overhaul trigger system and message conversion
target.body:
> **Draft / work-in-progress** — this PR is under active development. Feedback welcome on the overall direction.

## Summary

Introduce a **trajectory-first, trigger-driven training export system** that produces episode-level JSONL data from the OpenClaw runtime — no offline reconstruction, no separate pipeline. The system is **opt-in** (`trainingExport.enabled: true`) and writes to:

```
~/.openclaw/training-export/episodes.jsonl
```

Each line is a self-contained training sample: a **task episode** (full agent turn with system prompt, messages, tools, metadata) or a **compact-summary episode** (compression prompt → summary pair for RL compaction training).

## Relationship to Existing Systems

### `/export-trajectory` (human-facing debug bundles)

The existing `/export-trajectory` command (docs at `docs/tools/trajectory.md`) produces **redacted interactive support bundles** for human debugging — prompt timelines, tool traces, transcript snapshots, usage metadata. It is triggered manually by users or support staff.

The training export introduced here is **complementary and non-overlapping**:

| | `/export-trajectory` | Training Export |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Human debugging, support | Machine training data |
| **Trigger** | Manual command | Automatic (compaction, reset, export command) |
| **Output** | Redacted bundle directory | JSONL episodes |
| **Format** | Directory of text/markdown files | One JSON line per episode |
| **Privacy** | Redacted (best-effort) | Full content (machine-consumed; opt-in) |
| **Audience** | Developers, support | RL training pipelines |

Both systems **read from the same trajectory** (trajectory capture / `cache-trace`). Training export simply produces a different output format for a different consumer, alongside the existing mechanism.

### Compaction subsystem

Training export hooks into the Pi SDK compaction lifecycle (`session_before_compact` + `session_compact`) to capture:
- **Pre-compaction context** (task episode): the full conversation before compression
- **Post-compaction summary** (summary episode): the prompt sent to the summarization model + the summary it produced, with `compaction` metadata (`tokensBefore`, `firstKeptEntryId`, `fromExtension`)

This is the same data the compaction system already computes internally — training export just persists it in a structured training format before it is discarded.

## Key Design Decisions

### 1. Trajectory-first
All training fields (system prompt, messages, tools, model metadata) come from runtime trajectory `context.compiled` events. Message and tool conversion delegates to the Pi SDK / provider layer (`convertMessages` from `@mariozechner/pi-ai/openai-completions`).

### 2. Unified compaction hook
A single Pi SDK extension (`session_before_compact` + `session_compact`) handles **all** compaction modes (default, safeguard, manual, overflow, timeout). No `runTrainingExport` calls scattered across individual compaction paths.

### 3. Pair-export guarantee
For compaction-triggered exports, task and summary episodes are built as a batch. If either is filtered by quality checks, **the entire batch is discarded** — no orphaned episodes.

### 4. Config-gated at every call site
`getTrainingExportConfig(cfg)?.enabled === true` is checked at all three entry points (extension registration, session reset, trajectory export command), so reviewers can see the opt-in gating logic without digging into implementation details.

### 5. `compactionSummary` bridging
Pi SDK's `convertToLlm` converts `compactionSummary` → `user` messages, but the upstream `convertMessages` from `@mariozechner/pi-ai/openai-completions` does not handle the `compactionSummary` role. A pre-processing step (sharing a single map with thinking-block stripping) mirrors Pi SDK's conversion format before handing off to the upstream converter.

### 6. Training-quality message filtering (all triggers)
Training episodes must end with a complete `assistant` message — regardless of trigger type. Any snapshot (compaction, reset, or trajectory export) may end mid-turn at a non-`assistant` message (e.g. `toolResult`). Trailing non-assistant messages are trimmed from every trigger's output. The `trainExampleMessagesAreUsable` check requires ≥1 user + ≥1 assistant; if trimming leaves the episode unusable, it is discarded entirely. This is a universal training-data quality requirement, not a compaction-specific behavior.

### 7. Reset export is independent of plugin hooks
The `before_reset` training export call is placed **outside** `emitGatewayBeforeResetPluginHook`, so it fires regardless of whether any `before_reset` plugin hooks are registered.

### 8. Private file permissions
The export directory (`~/.openclaw/training-export`) and JSONL file are created with private filesystem modes (`0o700` / `0o600`) to prevent world-readable access to training data.

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/training-export.ts` | **New** — core module: snapshot collection, episode construction, JSONL I/O, prompt constants, compaction extension |
| `src/training-export.test.ts` | **New** — test suite |
| `docs/training-export.md` | **New** — formal feature documentation |
| `src/config/types.openclaw.ts` | Add `trainingExport` config type (`enabled`, `compat`) |
| `src/config/zod-schema.ts` | Add `trainingExport` schema |
| `src/config/schema.help.ts` | Add field help text |
| `src/config/schema.labels.ts` | Add field labels |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts` | Register compaction extension (config-gated, opt-in) |
| `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts` | `before_reset` trigger (config-gated, outside hook function) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-export-trajectory.ts` | `trajectory_export` trigger (config-gated, alongside existing command) |
| `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` | Minor: export `convertResponsesMessages` for use in conversion pipeline |

## Configuration

```yaml
trainingExport:
  enabled: true            # default: false (opt-in)
  compat: {}               # optional ModelCompatConfig override for export path
```

When `enabled` is `false` (the default), the extension is not registered and `runTrainingExport` is never called — zero overhead.

## How to Test

1. Enable via `trainingExport.enabled: true`
2. Trigger a compaction in a session long enough to exceed the context threshold
3. Check `~/.openclaw/training-export/episodes.jsonl` — should contain paired task + summary episodes with `compaction` metadata
4. Reset a session — should produce a task episode
5. Run `/export-trajectory` — should produce a task episode via the training export path as well
6. Disable via `trainingExport.enabled: false` — episodes file should receive no new entries

## Open Questions for Review

1. **Default to opt-in (`enabled: false`)** — is this the right default, or should we consider a different approach?
2. **Privacy and retention policy** — the training export writes full (non-redacted) session content to disk. Should there be a retention/cleanup mechanism?

target.title: fix(ios): classify TLS fingerprint timeouts
target.body:
Related: OpenClaw claim report ACTIONABLE-003-C2

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where iOS users connecting to a manual HTTPS gateway could get stuck behind generic connection failure behavior when the gateway accepts TCP but never completes enough TLS handshake work to produce a certificate fingerprint.

This also fixes the root alert-stack breakage where the gateway trust prompt could be prepared but not presented from the app's main root UI.

## Why This Change Was Made

The iOS gateway connector now classifies TLS fingerprint probing outcomes separately from plain TCP reachability, including a bounded TLS fingerprint timeout for secure endpoints that stall after TCP connect. The trust prompt presentation was also moved to SwiftUI's current `alert(_:isPresented:presenting:actions:message:)` API so it survives the root prompt stack alongside deep-link prompts.

The change keeps the timeout decision inside the gateway connection controller instead of adding settings-screen heuristics or widening generic connection fallbacks.

## User Impact

Users now see a specific TLS fingerprint timeout message when a secure gateway endpoint is reachable but does not complete fingerprint verification in time. First-time TLS connections can again present the trust prompt from the main iOS app shell, so users can inspect and trust the gateway fingerprint instead of seeing a silent or misleading connection failure.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR.

- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` reported no accepted/actionable findings before commit.
- Focused XCTest before rebasing onto current `origin/main`: `xcodebuild test -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 17,OS=26.5' -only-testing:OpenClawTests/SwiftUIRenderSmokeTests -only-testing:OpenClawTests/RootTabsSourceGuardTests -only-testing:OpenClawTests/GatewayConnectionSecurityTests` passed 55 tests.
- Live simulator proof before rebasing: a local self-signed TLS endpoint on `127.0.0.1:29664` produced the `Trust this gateway?` prompt with SHA-256 fingerprint and `Trust and connect` / `Cancel` actions.
- Live simulator regression proof before rebasing: a local stalled TCP endpoint on `127.0.0.1:29664` produced `TLS fingerprint verification timed out for 127.0.0.1:29664. Secure endpoint was reached, but TLS did not finish in time.`
- After rebasing onto current `origin/main`, regenerated the ignored local Xcode project with `xcodegen generate` and confirmed the branch builds with `xcodebuild build -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 17 Pro,OS=26.5'`.
- Post-reset focused XCTest on the amended branch: `xcodebuild test -project OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 17 Pro,OS=26.5' -only-testing:OpenClawTests/GatewayConnectionSecurityTests` passed 12 tests.
- A later combined local focused run rebuilt successfully, launched the simulator app, then hung in Xcode/CoreSimulator before materializing the test worker (`waiting for workers to materialize`, `Waiting for -runningDidFinish call`). No test assertions failed in that run.
- `swiftlint lint Sources/Design/SettingsProTabActions.swift` passed with 0 violations.
- PR CI on `f2a72a02ef295a2c60990b49086c9264974e8703`: `ios-build`, `macos-swift`, and `Scan iOS dead code` passed.

target.title: feat(gateway): support hidden chat sends
target.body:
## Summary
- add `chat.send.hideUserMessage` to the Gateway protocol schema and Swift protocol model
- require `operator.admin` before a hidden send can suppress the triggering user turn
- reject hidden sends with `deliver: true` or attachments so the hidden path stays narrow
- keep assistant output/history behavior intact while skipping only the user transcript write

## Why
This is the small protocol/Gateway foundation for hidden bootstrap turns. It does not add URL autostart behavior; that is intentionally split into follow-up #79653.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `chat.send` accepts `hideUserMessage: true` for admin-scoped callers, does not record the triggering user prompt in chat history, and rejects attachments on the hidden path.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS source checkout at `a1884362174eaf72cae7b2ddcbd2430c7067a736`, foreground OpenClaw Gateway on `ws://127.0.0.1:19886` with token auth and `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started a real gateway with `pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19886 --bind loopback --auth token --token <redacted> --ws-log compact`, connected over the real Gateway WebSocket with admin/read/write operator scopes, called `chat.history`, called `chat.send` with `{ "sessionKey": "main", "message": "/context list", "hideUserMessage": true }`, called `chat.history` again, then attempted a hidden send with an image attachment.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the live local gateway client run:

```json
{
  "connected": true,
  "url": "ws://127.0.0.1:19886",
  "before": {
    "sessionId": null,
    "messageCount": 0,
    "texts": []
  },
  "send": {
    "runId": "proof-hidden-send-1778301902610",
    "status": "started"
  },
  "after": {
    "sessionId": "933b48d9-d050-4337-b09f-291cd535db91",
    "messageCount": 0,
    "texts": [],
    "containsHiddenPrompt": false
  },
  "attachmentGuard": {
    "ok": false,
    "message": "hideUserMessage cannot be combined with attachments"
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The real gateway accepted the hidden send, returned a backing `sessionId` on the next history load, did not expose `/context list` in chat history, and rejected hidden sends with attachments.
- **What was not tested**: Browser URL autostart; that is split into dependent follow-up #79653.

## Testing
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send can hide the triggering user turn from chat history|chat.send requires operator.admin to hide the triggering user turn|chat.send rejects hideUserMessage with attachments"`
